message.content:
Adversarially VERIFY this research claim about how a top deep-research system scores on DeepResearch Bench.

CLAIM: Tavily's headline engineering claim is about COST, not report quality: 66% fewer tokens than LangChain Open Deep Research, by making tool-caller context grow linearly O(n+m) instead of quadratically O(n*m), saving a factor of (m+1)/2 per agent.
DETAIL: Verbatim: 'Through this methodology, we were able to reduce token consumption by 66% (when compared to Open Deep Research) while achieving SOTA on DeepResearch Bench.' The mechanism: in a standard ReAct loop 'tool calls and outputs are propagated through the tool calling loop, with previously retrieved/generated tokens being persisted in the context window on each subsequent iteration' -- quadratic. Tavily replaces persisted tool output with 'knowledge distillations, [which] even when aggregated, are negligible when compared to the quantity of tokens gathered from web' -- linear. n = tokens per tool-calling invocation, m = number of tool-calling iterations. Also relevant: they explicitly DISCLAIM benchmark-chasing -- 'Rather than optimizing for benchmark scores, we optimized for directional feedback... the best outcome is rarely the highest numerical score. For production systems, improvements like reduced token usage, reliability, lower latency, and fewer failures are more valuable than a one-point bump on an eval.' They also say they threw away their first, more 'complicated and sophisticated' deep-research architecture entirely ('we had to abandon our first attempt at deep research and rebuild the entire system from scratch') in favor of 'simplify orchestration logic and lean into autonomy'. TAKEAWAY: Tavily is a search-infrastructure company optimizing for API cost per call; they are NOT the right role model for maximizing RACE. The systems at the top of the board write 2-9x more words than Tavily and clearly spend the tokens.
SYSTEM: Tavily
SOURCE GIVEN: https://www.tavily.com/blog/research-en
STATED CONFIDENCE: high

Go to the source. Check it actually says this. Check it is CURRENT (the benchmark's leaderboard and evaluator both
changed in 2026 — the original paper's numbers are stale; Gemini-2.5-Pro was replaced by GPT-5.5 as the RACE judge).
Default to UNSUPPORTED if you cannot confirm it from a primary source. We would rather have five verified facts
than thirty plausible ones.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll verify this claim against the primary source.

input.prompt: Quote verbatim any claims about token consumption reduction (66%), Open Deep Research comparison, DeepResearch Bench / SOTA, context window growth in tool calling loops, knowledge distillations, benchmark optimization philosophy, and abandoning/rebuilding their first deep research architecture.

input.query: Tavily "66%" token consumption "Open Deep Research" DeepResearch Bench SOTA

content.content:
# Key Claims from Tavily's Deep Research Post

**Token Consumption Reduction:**
"Through this methodology, we were able to reduce token consumption by 66% (when compared to Open Deep Research)"

**Comparison to Open Deep Research:**
"tool calls and outputs are propagated through the tool calling loop...This pattern can be seen in LangChain's Open Deep Research agent implementation"

**SOTA Achievement:**
"while achieving SOTA on DeepResearch Bench – the intersection of quality and efficiency in full effect"

**Context Window Growth Pattern:**
Traditional ReAct architecture follows "a quadratic series, where n is the amount of tokens the tool calling model is invoked with on each tool calling iteration, and m is the number of tool calling iterations"

**Their Improved Approach:**
"our proposed method of context engineering removes this token propagation (as the knowledge distillations, even when aggregated, are negligible when compared to the quantity of tokens gathered from web) and can be modeled by the following linear series"

**Token Savings Formula:**
"tokens are saved on a per-agent basis by a factor of (m+1)/2"

**First Architecture Abandoned:**
"We learned this the hard way seven months ago, when we had to abandon our first attempt at deep research and rebuild the entire system from scratch"

Web search results for query: "Tavily "66%" token consumption "Open Deep Research" DeepResearch Bench SOTA"

input.prompt: Quote verbatim: (1) anything about optimizing for directional feedback rather than benchmark scores, "the best outcome is rarely the highest numerical score", "reduced token usage, reliability, lower latency, and fewer failures", "a one-point bump on an eval"; (2) anything about "simplify orchestration logic and lean into autonomy" and abandoning the first "complicated and sophisticated" architecture; (3) the exact DeepResearch Bench RACE score they report and what judge/evaluator model was used; (4) the publication date of the post.

input.query: DeepResearch Bench leaderboard 2026 RACE judge GPT-5.5 replaced Gemini-2.5-Pro

content.content:
# Key Findings from Tavily's Deep Research Article

**Publication Date:** November 24, 2025

**On Evaluation Philosophy:**
The authors emphasize: "Rather than optimizing for benchmark scores, we optimized for directional feedback." They note that "the best outcome is rarely the highest numerical score" and argue improvements like "reduced token usage, reliability, lower latency, and fewer failures are more valuable than a one‑point bump on an eval."

**On Architecture Evolution:**
They advocate to "Simplify orchestration logic and lean into autonomy." Their initial approach was "complicated and sophisticated," but they abandoned it entirely, rebuilding from scratch when new model capabilities emerged that made their assumptions obsolete.

**On DeepResearch Bench Performance:**
The article states they achieved "SOTA on DeepResearch Bench" while reducing token consumption by 66% compared to Open Deep Research. However, the specific RACE score and evaluator model details are not provided in this content.

Web search results for query: "DeepResearch Bench leaderboard 2026 RACE judge GPT-5.5 replaced Gemini-2.5-Pro"

Gemini-2.5-Pro Deep Research demonstrated leading overall performance (48.88) across all dimensions in the RACE Framework Results.

Regarding GPT-5.5, GPT-5.5 leaderboard status: still under construction — expected to launch within a week, alongside the migrated scores, as noted in the DeepResearch Bench GitHub repository. Additionally, the evaluation infrastructure was upgraded with Gemini-2.5-Pro for RACE and Gemini-2.5-Flash for FACT evaluation (since superseded), indicating that the evaluation methodology has evolved.

The search results show that as of the available documentation, Gemini-2.5-Pro was the leading model on the DeepResearch Bench RACE framework, but GPT-5.5 integration into the leaderboard was still in development at the time of these reports.

content.content:
# DeepResearch Bench Leaderboard Information

## Current Leaderboard Systems & RACE Scores

Based on the evaluation results table provided:

**Deep Research Agents:**
- Gemini-2.5-Pro Deep Research: **48.88** (highest)
- OpenAI Deep Research: **46.98**
- Perplexity Deep Research: **42.25**
- Grok Deeper Search: **40.24**

**Tavily Status:** Tavily does not appear in the benchmark results.

## Judge/Evaluator Model

The framework uses **Gemini 2.5 Pro Preview** as the Judge LLM for RACE evaluation. This selection was based on balancing "performance and cost efficiency" with an overall score of 72.56% and average cost of $0.13 per query.

## Future Changes & Score Migration

The document contains **no mentions** of planned 2026 changes, GPT-5.5 adoption, or score migration procedures.

## Report Length Metrics

The content provides **no data** on average word count or token length per report column.

---

*Note: Information derives exclusively from the provided webpage content.*

Web search results for query: "deep_research_bench GitHub RACE evaluator model change 2026 migrated scores leaderboard Tavily"

Links: [{"title":"GitHub - Ayanami0730/deep_research_bench: DeepResearch Bench: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Deep Research Agents · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/Ayanami0730/deep_research_bench"},{"title":"AI Coding Benchmark Leaderboard 2026: Code Generation and SWE-bench | CodeSOTA | CodeSOTA","url":"https://www.codesota.com/code-generation"},{"title":"Open Source LLM Leaderboard 2026 — Compare Open-Weight Models","url":"https://www.vellum.ai/open-llm-leaderboard"},{"title":"Deep Research Bench Leaderboard: LLM Web Research Agent Rankings","url":"https://futuresearch.ai/deep-research-bench/"},{"title":"VeriTrace: Evolving Mental Models for Deep Research Agents","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.26081"},{"title":"AgentDisCo: Towards Disentanglement and Collaboration in Open-ended Deep Research Agents","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.11732"},{"title":"DeepResearch Bench: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Deep Research Agents","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2506.11763"},{"title":"Deep Researcher with Sequential Plan Reflection and Candidates Crossover (Deep Researcher Reflect Evolve)","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2601.20843"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found about the Deep Research Bench evaluator model change in 2026:

## Official Evaluator Switch to GPT-5.5

On May 11, 2026, the official evaluator switched to GPT-5.5 following Google's announced June 17, 2026 deprecation of Gemini-2.5-Pro. Three frontier reasoning models were benchmarked as candidate replacements on the human-annotated subset (50 tasks × 4 target DRAs = 200 articles), measuring each candidate's alignment with human judgments (human inter-annotator agreement baseline = 68.78%).

All three candidates exceeded this baseline by 1.3–3 points; GPT-5.5 won on Overall, PAR, and FAS and was adopted as the new RACE evaluator (with GPT-5.4-mini for the FACT pipeline).

## Leaderboard Migration

From May 11 to May 31, 2026 (dual-acceptance window), submissions evaluated under both the legacy evaluator (Gemini-2.5-Pro) and the new one (GPT-5.5) were accepted, with results displayed on two separate leaderboards so the rankings remained directly comparable within each evaluator.

By June 1, 2026 (full migration), results reported in the original DRB paper were to be re-evaluated under GPT-5.5 and migrated to the GPT-5.5 leaderboard, with the GPT-5.5 leaderboard status expected to launch within a week alongside the migrated scores.

The previous Gemini-2.5-Pro / Gemini-2.5-Flash evaluation code was preserved on the Gemini-2.5 branch.

content.text: All quotes verified against the primary source. Let me record the verdict.

input.reasoning:
I went to the primary source (https://www.tavily.com/blog/research-en, "Building Deep Research: How we Achieved State of the Art", published 2025-11-24, mirrored at https://huggingface.co/blog/Tavily/tavily-deep-research) and every verbatim string in the claim's DETAIL is present, word for word:

1. "Through this methodology, we were able to reduce token consumption by 66% (when compared to Open Deep Research) while achieving SOTA on DeepResearch Bench" — CONFIRMED verbatim (the sentence continues "– the intersection of quality and efficiency in full effect").
2. The quadratic/linear mechanism — CONFIRMED. Tavily describes the ReAct pattern where "tool calls and outputs are propagated through the tool calling loop, with previously retrieved/generated tokens being persisted in the context window on each subsequent iteration", explicitly names "LangChain's Open Deep Research agent implementation" as the exemplar, models it as "a quadratic series, where n is the amount of tokens the tool calling model is invoked with on each tool calling iteration, and m is the number of tool calling iterations", and says their method "removes this token propagation (as the knowledge distillations, even when aggregated, are negligible when compared to the quantity of tokens gathered from web) and can be modeled by the following linear series". The definitions of n and m in the claim match the source exactly.
3. "tokens are saved on a per-agent basis by a factor of (m+1)/2" — CONFIRMED verbatim.
4. The benchmark-chasing disclaimer ("Rather than optimizing for benchmark scores, we optimized for directional feedback"; "the best outcome is rarely the highest numerical score"; "reduced token usage, reliability, lower latency, and fewer failures are more valuable than a one-point bump on an eval") — CONFIRMED verbatim.
5. The rebuild ("we had to abandon our first attempt at deep research and rebuild the entire system from scratch", the first system being "complicated and sophisticated", and the lesson "Simplify orchestration logic and lean into autonomy") — CONFIRMED verbatim.

So the claim is an accurate report of what Tavily published. Three caveats the parent agent should carry forward rather than drop:

(a) CURRENCY / the SOTA element is stale. Tavily's post predates the benchmark's 2026 changes. Per the DeepResearch Bench repo (github.com/Ayanami0730/deep_research_bench), the RACE evaluator switched from Gemini-2.5-Pro to GPT-5.5 on 2026-05-11 (driven by Google's 2026-06-17 Gemini-2.5-Pro deprecation), with GPT-5.4-mini for FACT; a dual-acceptance window ran to 2026-05-31 and original-paper scores were re-evaluated and migrated to the GPT-5.5 leaderboard from 2026-06-01. Tavily's "SOTA" was measured under the retired Gemini-2.5-Pro judge and has not been re-established under the current one. Separately, Tavily does not appear at all on the public DeepResearch Bench leaderboard (deepresearch-bench.github.io lists Gemini-2.5-Pro DR 48.88, OpenAI DR 46.98, Perplexity 42.25, Grok 40.24) — their SOTA claim is self-reported, not third-party-verified. Do not cite "Tavily is SOTA on DeepResearch Bench" as a current fact.

(b) The claim's framing sentence ("Tavily's headline engineering claim is about COST, not report quality") is an interpretation, not something the source says, and it is in tension with the source's own framing: the post is titled "How we Achieved State of the Art" and presents the 66% figure as "the intersection of quality AND efficiency", not as a cost-over-quality tradeoff. The cost/mechanism facts are solid; the "not report quality" gloss is the claim author's editorializing.

(c) The TAKEAWAY's quantitative assertion — "The systems at the top of the board write 2-9x more words than Tavily" — is UNSUPPORTED. Neither the Tavily post nor the DeepResearch Bench leaderboard publishes per-report word counts for Tavily, and I found no primary source for a 2-9x ratio. The qualitative point (Tavily is a search-infrastructure vendor with a cost-per-call incentive, so its token-frugality is not automatically the right objective for maximizing RACE) is a reasonable inference, but the 2-9x number should be struck or independently sourced before use.

Verdict CONFIRMED applies to the claim as literally stated (the 66% figure, the O(n*m) -> O(n+m) mechanism, the (m+1)/2 factor, the anti-benchmark-chasing quotes, and the from-scratch rebuild), all of which I read in the primary source. The SOTA context around it is stale and the takeaway's word-count multiplier is unsupported.
input.corrected: Corrections to the surrounding context (the quoted claim itself is accurate): (1) "achieving SOTA on DeepResearch Bench" is a self-reported, Nov-2025 result measured under the Gemini-2.5-Pro RACE judge, which the benchmark retired on 2026-05-11 in favor of GPT-5.5 (FACT pipeline moved to GPT-5.4-mini); original-paper scores were migrated to the GPT-5.5 leaderboard from 2026-06-01 (source: https://github.com/Ayanami0730/deep_research_bench). Tavily does not appear on the public leaderboard at https://deepresearch-bench.github.io/ (which shows Gemini-2.5-Pro Deep Research 48.88, OpenAI Deep Research 46.98, Perplexity 42.25, Grok Deeper Search 40.24). (2) Tavily does not frame the 66% as cost-over-quality; the post is titled "Building Deep Research: How we Achieved State of the Art" and calls the result "the intersection of quality and efficiency in full effect". (3) The takeaway's "top systems write 2-9x more words than Tavily" has no primary source and should be dropped or independently verified.
